# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--04--24_06:39:29-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 169 English News Articles published between 2026-04-17 and 2026-04-24.*

## 1. Cabinet approved an expert panel to review and revive thousands of stalled housing projects under the National Housing Development Authority.

The portfolio recorded 64,407 units planned between 2015 and 2019, but only 10,532 had been delivered, leaving roughly 84% of the target incomplete. Authorities estimated Rs. 11.26 billion would be needed to finish the backlog, equivalent to under 0.05% of 2025 GDP, to lift delivery from about one in six planned homes to full completion.

## 2. Sri Lanka established a committee under the Presidential Secretariat to track implementation of post‑Easter attack probes.

The mechanism covered recommendations from a Presidential Commission of Inquiry and a former Sectoral Oversight Committee, replacing ad hoc follow‑up with a standing process. The move introduced formal progress monitoring seven years after the 2019 bombings, shifting fragmented oversight into a single administrative channel.

## 3. A Colombo court extended detention in the Easter case as a former State Intelligence chief remained in custody under the Prevention of Terrorism Act.

The magistrate directed investigators to file a progress report by 20 May, maintaining detention during ongoing inquiry steps. The production order followed the suspect’s February arrest and designation as the third person named in the case file.

## 4. India’s Vice President visited Colombo for talks that put energy connectivity and port development at the forefront.

Projects under discussion included a hub at Trincomalee and a proposed cross‑border oil pipeline, alongside housing schemes financed under a previously announced $450 million package for post‑cyclone works. The visit maintained recent high‑level exchanges as both sides worked on economic and digital cooperation amid rising regional energy security concerns.

## 5. SriLankan Airlines awarded Dubai ground handling to Dnata in a three‑year deal covering passenger, ramp and cargo services.

The agreement ran from 1 April 2026 for passenger and ramp services at an estimated AED 33.04 million and from 1 July 2026 for cargo at AED 7.6 million, following the expiry of the prior standard agreements in March and June 2026. The carrier continued scheduled passenger and freight operations at Dubai International under the new contracts in a single‑provider airport environment.

## 6. Business surveys showed broad‑based expansion in March as both manufacturing and services PMI readings improved.

The factory index rose to 66.7 from 56.8 in February, with new orders at 69.9 and firms building input stocks despite lengthening supplier lead times. The services gauge climbed to 59.4 from 54.4, underpinned by finance, wholesale and retail activity, while employment increased and expectations for the next quarter remained above the neutral threshold.

## 7. A UNDP assessment put the potential economic impact of the Middle East war on Asia‑Pacific at up to $299 billion.

The preliminary estimate equated to 0.3%–0.8% of regional GDP and included a projection that 8.8 million people could fall into poverty under severe scenarios. The analysis highlighted rapid pass‑through of higher fuel and freight costs to transport, electricity, food and fertilizer prices in a region where over 80% of crude and LNG flows through sensitive chokepoints.

## 8. The Central Bank confirmed National Development Bank’s engagement of overseas forensic experts in a Rs. 13.2 billion internal fraud probe.

The financial regulator retained direct oversight of the investigation, which also required an independent review of policies, systems and internal controls at the institution. The bank remained compliant with capital and liquidity requirements during the inquiry, as the supervisor emphasized reporting of interim findings to ensure depositor protection and system stability.

## 9. The Government confirmed Iraj Chaminda Pathiraja as Controller General of Immigration and Emigration on a permanent basis.

The appointment followed nearly a year of acting service in the role after Cabinet approval in May 2025. The move formalized leadership at a critical border‑control agency amid elevated movement volumes that exceeded pre‑pandemic levels during 2025, according to tourism and labour data.

## 10. JXG’s Rs. 5 billion initial public offering was over three times subscribed on day one, drawing 20,397 applications worth Rs. 15.16 billion.

The issue offered 500 million voting shares at Rs. 10 each, representing a 21.74% post‑issue stake in the company, and marked the largest primary equity raise in nearly 15 years. Proceeds were earmarked with Rs. 3.5 billion for expansion across general insurance, microfinance and non‑bank finance, Rs. 500 million for regional growth, and Rs. 1 billion for debt reduction to improve capital flexibility.
